questions.question: Prove that the lengths of tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal. Using this result, if a quadrilateral $ABCD$ is drawn to circumscribe a circle, prove that $AB + CD = AD + BC$.

steps: State the theorem: Tangents from an external point are equal.
Identify the points of contact $P, Q, R, S$ on sides $AB, BC, CD, DA$ respectively.
Express $AP=AS, BP=BQ, CQ=CR, DR=DS$.
Sum the left and right sides of these equations.
Group the terms to form the sides of the quadrilateral.
solution.derivation: $AP = AS, \ BP = BQ, \ CQ = CR, \ DR = DS \ (Tangents \ from \ external \ point) \\ AB + CD = (AP + PB) + (CR + RD) \\ = (AS + BQ) + (CQ + DS) \\ = (AS + DS) + (BQ + CQ) \\ = AD + BC$

questions.question: Two concentric circles are of radii $5 \text{ cm}$ and $3 \text{ cm}$. Find the length of the chord of the larger circle which touches the smaller circle.

steps: Let $O$ be the center, $AB$ be the chord of the larger circle, and $P$ be the point of contact on the smaller circle.
Radius $OP = 3 \text{ cm}$ is perpendicular to chord $AB$.
Radius $OA = 5 \text{ cm}$ is the hypotenuse of right $\triangle OPA$.
Use Pythagoras theorem: $AP^2 + OP^2 = OA^2$.
Calculate $AP = \sqrt{5^2 - 3^2} = 4 \text{ cm}$.
Since the perpendicular from center bisects the chord, $AB = 2 \times AP = 8 \text{ cm}$.
solution.derivation: $OP \perp AB \Rightarrow \angle OPA = 90^\circ \\ AP = \sqrt{OA^2 - OP^2} \\ AP = \sqrt{5^2 - 3^2} = \sqrt{25 - 9} = \sqrt{16} = 4 \text{ cm} \\ AB = 2 \times AP = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ cm}$

diagram.caption: Chord of larger circle touching smaller circle

questions.question: A circle is inscribed in a $\triangle ABC$ having sides $8 \text{ cm}, 10 \text{ cm}$ and $12 \text{ cm}$. Find the lengths of the segments $AD, BE, CF$ where $D, E, F$ are points of contact on sides $AB, BC, CA$ respectively.

steps: Let $AD = AF = x, BD = BE = y, CE = CF = z$.
Write equations: $x + y = 8, y + z = 10, z + x = 12$.
Sum the equations: $2(x + y + z) = 30 \Rightarrow x + y + z = 15$.
Solve for $x, y, z$ by subtracting original equations from sum.

questions.question: Prove that the tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.

steps: Draw a circle with center $O$ and tangent $XY$ at point $P$.
Take a point $Q$ on $XY$ other than $P$. Join $OQ$.
Since $Q$ is outside, $OQ > OP$.
This implies $OP$ is the shortest distance from $O$ to $XY$.
The shortest distance from a point to a line is the perpendicular distance, so $OP \perp XY$.
solution.derivation: $Let \ XY \ be \ the \ tangent \ at \ P. \ Take \ point \ Q \ on \ XY, \ Q \neq P. \\ OQ > OP \ (since \ Q \ is \ outside \ the \ circle). \\ OP \ is \ the \ shortest \ distance \ from \ O \ to \ line \ XY. \\ Therefore, \ OP \perp XY.$
